entries.form_section: LJLA TargJob 2x as an independent word <aram> bmw </aram> replicating the biblical source; whether or not the Aramaic is sensitive to any special meaning of the Biblical form is impossible to say for certain, but one may guess it may have been intended to mean something like "with my very own".
entries.end_notes:
For the common prepositional and adverbial usages see under the collocations. N.B.: The decision to list one or another of these collocations as a separate lemma is not always a straightforward one.
For the meaning "with" do not confuse instrumental b_ with locative ˁm.
in some LJLA mss. one finds a form בי, both as an independent and proclitic before the third person pronoun in the sense "in that one". This is to be seen not as based on the JBA form of בין, but rather a real phonological development from bhāhī -> bayhī, etc. The JBA form, on the other hand, is surely responsible for the scribal tendency to spell it as a separate word. In any case this usage is to be kept separate from those instances where the yodh is a simple vowel letter.

Hebrew and/or Arabic; see also s.v. bhmwt. The Mandaic is understood as "ass" in recent times (see MD), but given the common usage of ḥmr #2 n.m./f. for that meaning in Mandaic, it is more likely originally to have referred to larger domesticated animals, i.e. oxen or buffaloes.
The CPA usage in the cited passage is rejected in DCPA p. 100 s.v. hymˀ; however the connotations of the root in Arabic include "difficulty, anxiety" and the like, so there is ample reason to maintain the reading and interpretation presented here. Compare s.v. bhm adj.

Some have suggested that this root is a by-form of bsy; others that it is a weakening of bzḥ.
In the Palestinian contexts the nature of the disgrace would seem to refer to public sexual display.
At Pesh. Mal. 1:6 this root in the MT is twice rendered by forms understood to be from bzz "to despoil", but it is quite likely that they are remnants of this root.

entries.form_section: const. bēṯ > bē in many compounds; pl. bāttīn, etc.; Mand: baitia; JLA abs. bA) in the expression bA) (al bA) (TgIs 5:8); in Jewish texts the absolute is often
entries.end_notes:
byt under Hebrew influence. This word is used in both major meanings "structure/house" and "place, location" in many compounds. Here, in contrast with many previous writers, we have separated out as independent lemmas only those meanings wherein a distinct meaning has developed, especially those verified by attestation in several dialects. Thus examples like by twryn and by tbn, "cattle barn" and "straw shed," are not considered to be lemmas unless there is a distinct difference in meaning between the "compound" using the construct state on the first word and the same expression using the d_ genitive on the second word.
In all meanings, but especially as "place", a prefixed b_ "in" is often to be understood but not written, resulting in the development of byt prep. "between"; for which see the note there.
For a more detailed discussion of its use as a section of text, see STS p. 47.

entries.primary_gloss: in the possession of; by means of
entries.end_notes:
In the meaning "by means of" in Syriac this form is often replaced by the more recent ܒܐܝܕܝ or sg. ܒܐܝܕ, often but not necessarily in cases where the literal semantic context of "hand" is still somewhat present. Forms may also be alternated for literary effect, for an example of which see 1Mac7a1 2:7. Note, too, that in the targumim “by means of” never refers to inanimates.
See also s.v. bˀydˀ prep.

entries.form_section: In Syriac often treated as a pure loanword, i.e., using the absolute form:  <syr>bEymy bEym) </syr> or even  <syr>bym </syr>;  pl.  <syr> bEm)T) b)m)s b)m)T) </syr>.  In JBA Aramaized:  <aram>bymws) </aram>; CPA emph.  <cpa>by_m)</cpa>
entries.end_notes: See also s.v. bym, bymˀ; Greek Lehnw 150). In targumic texts used as a play on Biblical Hebrew במה, "shrine," itself being incorrectly then rendered in the early versions as "high place." Since the Greek itself would appear to be from West Semitic, the entire complex represents a lovely example of multiple mutual borrowings and reborrowings.

entries.form_section: The distribution of the forms in the Standard literary Aramaic tradition is as follows: The independent form is bēn, but bēnē (baynay) before plural pronominal suffixes. In PTA, LJLA, and the colloquial Babylonian dialects the back-formation byny becomes the normal independent form, although there is a tendency in LJLA and JBA to use the older form before singulars as well as in the Hebraizing reduplication.
entries.end_notes: In Syriac only baynay , and (as opposed to byt, bynt) it is used in our database in older texts almost always only when used in the byny ... l_ construction and in complementary distribution with byt when there are pronominal suffixes. In JLA the 1st. sg. pron. suffix form is bEyna) See also s.vv. byt prep. and bynt prep. For Marqe 1.258 מלבין כל בסר see s.v. lbn#2 vb.

entries.form_section: takes sg. pron. suffixes inasmuch as it is a fem. plural form
entries.end_notes:
See also s.v. byt, byny. Note that in LJLA it occurs only in TargProv and one time each in TgPsJ and MegAntioch where the meaning is clearly "among".
This is the plural form of byt prep., and in many Syriac texts there is a clear distinction between the use of byt for "between" and bynt (usually with syāmē!) for "among", hence we have here separated the two. Elsewhere the two may be used in close context simply for variation.
In Mandaic this form appears to be used in complementary distribution with binia when used with pronominal suffixes.

entries.end_notes: From Akkadian birtu; see AIOA p. 44. For most cities the byrh would appear to have referred in general to the upper city, surrounded by its own wall, containing the administrative and religious centers. That it is the temple compound at Si' in the Nabataean is shown by the Greek parallel: τὸ ἱερόν.

entries.form_section: In the oldest Syriac texts  <syr>bEyt </syr> is used for both "between" and "among" while with pronominal suffixes its form is   <syr>bay:nay </syr>. Somewhat later many classic texts distinguish between this form as "between" while its plural form  <syr>bay:nAt </syr> is used for "among".
entries.end_notes:
See also s.v. byn, byny, bynt. Also see note s.v. bynt for LJLA.
The Mandaic usage bit, bit with d and before a verb is dubious. perhaps rather b_ p + d_ c !? But the form binat with pronominal suffixes is well attested.
It is often difficult to distinguish between this usage and the homophonous construct of "house" in Syriac; hence the origin of the usage as a variant of byn and bynt.
In the sole LJLA example, here cited, the first spelling is an error for the Syriac ܒܪܝܫ ܓܙܪܐ.. The second represents a typical example of the ambiguity in Syriac between "place of" and "among". Surely, though, the LJLA author understood the former.
The sole CPA example, as cited, is a Syriacism.

Zuckerman, "On being ‘damned certain’: the story of a curse in the Sefire inscription and its interpretation" in: Beck A, Bartelt A, Raabe P, Franke C (eds) Fortunate the eyes that see; essays in honor of David Noel Freedman in celebration of his 70th birthday. Eerdmans, Grand Rapids, MI, pp 422-435. But see also Brugnatelli, Henoch 17 (1995), especially in light of the following observation:
More interesting, perhaps, is the that this may be the etymon of the common Eastern Neo-Aramaic word baxta, "woman," as in Mandaic; but see s.v. bkth for the usually suggested alternative.

It is often suggested that this word (apparently in the absolute!) is the etymon of the common North-Eastern Neo-Aramaic word for woman (also in modern Mandaic), baxta; see G. Krotkoff, "Studies in Neo-Aramaic Lexicography," in A. Kort and S. Morschauser, eds., Biblical and Related Studies Presented to Samual Iwry (Eisenbrauns, Winona Lake: 1985), 131f.
Although there is no clear evidence, that this word exists in classical Mandaic in the meaning "lady" as argued by MD, the cited passage from Syriac for the gloss "working woman" already shows, (in the 5th century!) a path of development to the common NENA usage, although its clear meaning has been overlooked by previous lexicographers.

entries.end_notes: According to STS p. 53 there is a form ܒܝܕܘܟܬ which would seem to be a concise spelling of the longer form ܒܝܠܬ ܕܘܟܬ. Rather than the latter word being a separate divine name, we assume that it is simply the word "place" reflecting the earlier Assyrian practice of naming local Ishtars along with the town where they were worshipped, such as Ishtar of Arbela. Thus it should be rendered "local female goddess".

entries.extra_comments: MD’s bnita ‘building’ (MD 67a) results from a misreading of the manuscripts: urnitẖ (AM 150:19) = urnita (CS 26. 134a:4, Petermann 155. 176:7). This word must be identified with rnita (MD 436b) ‘pondering, meditation, reflection, anxiety, care, worry’, which appears an additional three times in Asfar Malwaši: 64:1, 178:15 and 201:4. The word bnita ‘building’ is not attested in Mandaic.

The Samaritan usages currently list s.v. bnn vb. #1 might equally be assigned to this lemma, i.e., derived from misbehaving children!
The Mandaic examples might equally be assigned to #1 and its own problematic identifications!

entries.end_notes: So treated as a lexeme by DJPA but dubious: only as a calque of MT: בְּפִי אַמְתַּחְתֹּ, the force of which is surely the fact that the object is secreted at the top of the saddle-bag, not just "inside" of it.

Meaning #2 is based on the biblical usage in Targum Onkelos of Deuteronomy rendering BH מַהֵר. The distinct meaning is clear in all LJLA examples without Onkelos parallels, and makes total sense from an etymological basis, but whether or not it should be ascribed as well to the JLA examples is uncertain.
See also s.v. bpwry.

See also s.v. pqˁh.
It is important to understand the difference in meaning between the terms "valley," "plain," and "mountain" in regions such as the Near East and the American West and the way they are typically used in European/American languages. A "valley" in such regions is often a vast flat "plain" between distant mountain ranges, whereas the term for "mountain" (e.g. Akkadian šadû) can correspond to the word "plateau" or "plain" when viewed from the other side, while in Europe one expects mountains to have peaks and valleys to be the deep areas between them.
For a possible semantic development in JBA see s.v. byqytˀ n.f.

entries.form_section: plural: bnīn (Sam. const. also bārī!); absolute: bar in literary and Eastern dialects, ber in Western (and occasional LJLA). In the plurals of compounds br almost always appears in the plural form itself. With pronominal suffixes in Syriac, the base is ber_ thus "my son" is simply bery .
entries.end_notes:
Occasionally, the Hebrew/Phoenician/Arabizing form בן is found in the sg.much as we might use bin or bar as part of a Semitic name in English contexts even today.
In Syriac the first syllable has elided vowel in suffixed forms but the vowel returns as /e/ (not /a/) when necessary. It is not clear whether this is the origin of the western absolute or not. Ancient cuneiform-written names in pi-ir should be understood to reflect the word "fruit" and not our word; there is no evidence for an original /i/ vowel in the Aramaic form.
Compounds are listed as independent headwords only when the meaning of the compound is other than the sum of its parts; use the "view entire list" option above to see the others, but note that some compounds share more than one of the listed semantic characteristics. It must also be understood that any compound in br could have an equivalent in brt- if the referent is explicitly femine.
The use of the plural to refer to "children" in general continues throughout the dialects, however the singular as "child" rather than explicitly "son" seems to be unattested after Official Aramaic, unless one assumes that an expression like בר דכר is not a tautology.

entries.end_notes: Traditionally and surely incorrectly "palace guard" or the like, as if from byrh n.f. "fortress", but DJBA’s "outlaws" is probably too misleading. From the context, clearly a major socio/political group that was controlling the access to Jerusalem, almost certainly a faction of those generally referred to as the "zealots". The precise a-political connotation of the term is uncertain, but the Akkadian parallel barānû, probably an Aramaic loan there, is generally rendered "rebel". See extended discussion in AIOA p. 41.

entries.end_notes: As always in the biblical tradition, in modern languages as well, this word means quite different things said of a human being or said of God. For a human it means something like "may you be blessed by God." when said of God or a name of God this word means "praised, bless-worthy, thanks to", presumably derived from something like "one whose name is worthy of being used in blessing mortals".

This Akkadian loanword (see AIOA 44) birītu commonly occurs natively only in the plural. A singular might be reconstructed as bī/ərīṯā, contra BarHebraeus (see LS2/Sok). Mandaic does adduce a back-formed singular: biria.
Another possible singular is the awkward form ברית at QumNJ 5:1:1:1 .

entries.form_section: LJLA bt n$) , pl. bnt n$) ; Mand. always pl. bnat anaša
entries.end_notes:
One must wonder if the LJLA usage is not rather from the colloquial pronunciation of npš.
The Mandaic expression bnat anaša with masculine concord has been studied by O. Abudraham (as yet unpublished, but see "https://www.academia.edu/44594627/Abudraham_The_Textual_Tradition_of_Classical_Mandaic_in_Light_of_the_Language_of_the_Epigraphic_Sources_One_Case_Study?auto=download&email_work_card=download-paper", who argues that it is a development from an earlier form bnit anaša, itself some kind of modification of bnia anaša. If so, though, we would suggest that bnit may have nothing to do with "sons" or "daughters", but is rather derived from bny vb.

entries.end_notes: In Indian astrology Saturn is considered the son of the Sun (so the notes in Drower's Book of the Zodiac p. 138). Furlani in ZDMG cited by and followed by LS takes the meaning to be a parhelion. The Syriac and Mandaic passages clearly refer to the same phenomenon -- indeed the text traditions are clearly related. The Mandaic passage could describe something like a parhelion, but we take it to be the son of the Sun, i.e. Saturn. However, in the Syriac of Bar Hebraeus, the expression "daughters" of the Sun, ܒܢ̈ܬ ܫܡܫܐ, clearly refers to parhelions (BHCandBak [116]4ff.).

DJBA, based on Lőw, defines this as Peganum harmala, i.e. Syrian rue. Given that the seeds of this plant are toxic and psychedelic (some think it is the source of Aryan haoma!), that is doubtful, since in our sources it is a strong but useful spice. Payne Smith's "wild rue" is a better suggestion.
The cited "other text" passage from TgJob for mng. 2 appears to have mis-remembered the Talmudic statement there cited and reversed the meaning of the word!

entries.form_section: w. plural pron. suffixes in most; but usually sg. in Syriac. OA: bˁšr; Qum btr; b)tr ; Mand: mostly abatar, for which see s.v. lbtr prep.
entries.end_notes:
It is difficult to pinpoint exactly when the literal meanings 'in place of' and 'in the footsteps of', clearly attested in OA and OfA, develop into the general prepositional sense. The early attestations could be translated either way. See, too, the note s.v. ˀḥry prep.
In Syriac the temporal usage has generally been transferred to mn btr, while alone the meaning is spacial.

entries.end_notes: The finite verbal form is as yet unattested, but now well attested in derived forms in Syriac magic bowls and Mandaic. Perhaps the entire complex is a by-form developed from bky, i.e. things that make one cry. On the other hand, given the occasional spelling of the noun with initial /n/ and its limitation to Babylonian magic, one cannot but think of a possible relationship to Akkadian nabalkutu "to overturn".
I-C Gazzola, "Lexique des termes liturgiques," in F. Cassingena-Trévedy and I. Jurasz, Les liturgies syriaques, p. 285.

The passage in Sirach cited by LS2 as the sole support of the meaning "to consume" for an otherwise non-existant pael is difficult. The Hebrew text has תעבה (NOT תבעה; see here), and the Syriac text might need to be emended accordingly with the meaning being "to make thick: i.e., to strengthen;" but compare treatment of this root in the Dictionary of Classical Hebrew entry בעה III.
